summary: alya-baileys hijacks installer WhatsApp accounts via typosquat C2 for auto-follow/auto-react fraud
details: This package is a Baileys-derived WhatsApp library that contains a remote-controlled abuse channel targeting any consumer who connects to WhatsApp through it. On every `connection.update == 'open'` event, lib/Socket/newsletter.js decrypts an embedded AES-256-CBC ciphertext (key derived from sha512('Alyabaileys_alyamoon')) and a hex-byte-concatenated string to recover the URL `https://raw.githubcontent-content.com` — a typosquat lookalike of GitHub's `raw.githubusercontent.com` content CDN. It then GETs a JSON config from this attacker-controlled host every 30 seconds and uses it to: (1) auto-follow arbitrary newsletter JIDs on the consumer's WhatsApp account via `_ensureFollow()`, (2) auto-react with attacker-chosen emojis to incoming messages from those JIDs via the `messages.upsert` handler, and (3) auto-vote on poll messages. The C2 URL is concealed via single-hex-byte `Buffer.from('68','hex')`-style concatenation specifically to evade string scanners — there is no legitimate engineering reason to disguise a config endpoint as GitHub's CDN. The consumer's WhatsApp account performs engagement actions they never authorized, controlled in real time by the package author. This constitutes account hijack and a remote-controlled backdoor channel that materially harms anyone deploying a bot built on this library.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
